rationale_for_baseline_tier: The most likely diagnosis is a benign acute panic attack, but the patient has ACTIVE palpitations with a subjective 'heart attack' sensation and mild breathlessness, on a background of subclinical Graves' disease (which raises atrial-fibrillation risk). A telehealth service cannot auscultate, take a blood pressure, or record an ECG, so it cannot exclude a tachyarrhythmia (SVT/rapid AF) or acute coronary syndrome — exactly the exclusions the source clinician made at the bedside to downgrade risk. The conservative, honest tier is therefore T3: urgent same-day in-person assessment with an ECG, rather than a routine referral (which would under-triage an unexcluded cardiac cause) or an emergency ambulance (which would over-triage a haemodynamically stable patient). The reassuring red-flag screen (no ischaemic chest pain, no syncope, afebrile, not delirious) supports same-day rather than emergency escalation.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Central, crushing or pressure-like chest pain, or pain spreading to the jaw, neck or arm, or profuse cold sweating
escalation_edges.rationale: These are features of acute coronary syndrome, a time-critical emergency requiring ambulance transport and hospital assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you get a tight, crushing or heavy pain in the centre of your chest, or pain spreading to your jaw, neck or arm, or you break out in a cold sweat — call 000 for an ambulance straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Fainting or near-fainting, or the heartbeat becoming chaotic, irregular or extremely fast
escalation_edges.rationale: Syncope or a chaotic/very fast rhythm suggests a haemodynamically significant tachyarrhythmia requiring emergency care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you feel like you are going to black out or actually faint, or your heartbeat suddenly becomes chaotic or extremely fast, call 000 immediately.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: High fever with confusion, agitation or feeling very unwell
escalation_edges.rationale: Fever with altered mental state in a patient with autoimmune thyroid disease raises the possibility of thyroid storm, a life-threatening emergency.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you develop a high fever along with confusion or feeling very unwell and agitated, call 000 — this needs emergency assessment.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Shortness of breath that persists or worsens even when you are calm and resting, or feeling faint
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Go to an emergency department the same day if this occurs
escalation_edges.rationale: Breathlessness that does not settle with calming is not typical of simple panic and warrants urgent in-person evaluation.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your breathlessness carries on or gets worse even when you have calmed down and are resting, or you feel faint, go to your nearest emergency department to be checked.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Palpitations continue, do not settle with breathing, or the patient remains highly distressed after paced breathing
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same-day in-person assessment with an ECG
escalation_edges.rationale: Baseline action for this case: because the rhythm cannot be characterised remotely, same-day assessment with an ECG is required to exclude a tachyarrhythmia and acute ischaemia.
escalation_edges.communication_template: Because we can't check your heart's rhythm over chat, I'd like you to be seen in person today for a quick heart tracing (ECG) to make sure your heart rhythm is safe — you can go to your GP, an urgent care clinic or after-hours service today.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Subclinical Graves' disease (positive TSH-receptor antibodies, suppressed TSH)
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Raises the baseline risk of atrial fibrillation, reinforcing the need for a same-day ECG rather than remote reassurance, and warrants ongoing GP thyroid monitoring.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: High health anxiety with compulsive pulse-checking
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Advice must validate the fear while avoiding both dismissal and over-escalation; over-investigation and an unnecessary ambulance would reinforce the anxiety cycle.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Your symptoms sound most like an acute anxiety/panic surge, and the reassuring answers you have given are helpful — but because I can't examine you or record your heart's rhythm over chat, I want you to be seen in person today for a quick heart tracing (ECG) to be sure your heart rhythm is safe. In the meantime, do the slow breathing exercise and try to stop checking your pulse.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: See a GP, urgent care clinic or after-hours service in person today for an ECG; call 000 if any of the emergency warning signs occur.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it is after hours, use an after-hours GP service or an urgent care clinic for the same-day ECG; do not wait days for a routine appointment.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the assessing clinician with the symptom timeline, the negative red-flag screen, the known subclinical Graves' status, and the request for an ECG to exclude a tachyarrhythmia and acute coronary syndrome.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Confidently labelling the episode a benign panic attack over telehealth and advising only self-care or a routine GP visit, without arranging any same-day in-person assessment or ECG.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: An unexcluded SVT/rapid atrial fibrillation or acute coronary syndrome is missed; the patient is falsely reassured and a treatable cardiac emergency evolves without assessment.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Deferring the cardiac assessment to a next-day/48-hour GP appointment while the patient still has active palpitations and an uncharacterised rhythm.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Dispatching an emergency ambulance (000) for a haemodynamically stable patient with a regular ~105 bpm tachycardia and no hard red flags.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether the rapid heartbeat is benign sinus tachycardia from panic or a tachyarrhythmia — unresolvable over telehealth.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Name the uncertainty honestly, treat the likely panic while arranging the same-day ECG that resolves it, and give explicit red-flag safety-netting.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Collapse the uncertainty prematurely into a confident remote 'benign panic' diagnosis, or over-escalate a stable patient to an ambulance.
